title: Word Order — V2 Rule & Verbs
situation: Foundation: the V2 rule, separable verbs, and verb-final subordinate clauses

phrases.hindi: Ik ga morgen naar Amsterdam. — Morgen ga ik naar Amsterdam.
phrases.english: I go tomorrow to Amsterdam. — Tomorrow, I go to Amsterdam.
phrases.context: The V2 (Verb Second) rule: in Dutch, the VERB must ALWAYS be in second position. When 'morgen' moves to position 1, the subject 'ik' flips to after the verb. The verb 'ga' stays in slot 2.
phrases.pronunciation: ik CHAA MOR-gen naar am-ster-DAM. — MOR-gen CHAA ik naar am-ster-DAM.

phrases.hindi: Nu eet ik. Thuis werk ik altijd. Gisteren was hij ziek.
phrases.english: Now I'm eating. At home I always work. Yesterday he was sick.
phrases.context: More V2 examples. Any element can go to position 1 (time, place, adverb), but the verb must stay second. This flipping of subject and verb is called 'inversion'.
phrases.pronunciation: nu AYT ik. TOWS WERK ik AL-tayt. CHIS-ter-en WAS hay ZEEK.

phrases.hindi: opstaan: Ik sta op. Ik sta om 7 uur op. Sta jij vroeg op?
phrases.english: to get up: I get up. I get up at 7. Do you get up early?
phrases.context: Separable verbs: 'op-staan' splits — the prefix 'op' goes to the END of the clause, the main verb stays second. 'Ik sta [om 7 uur] op' — 'op' always falls at the very end of the main clause.
phrases.pronunciation: op-STAAN: ik STAA op. ik STAA om ZEH-ven uur op. STAA yay VROOCH op?

phrases.hindi: bellen: ik bel op, aanzetten: ik zet aan, uitleggen: ik leg uit, meenemen: ik neem mee
phrases.english: to call (up): I call, to turn on: I turn on, to explain: I explain (lay out), to take along: I take along
phrases.context: Common separable verbs. Pattern: prefix + main verb. In present tense: main verb in position 2, prefix to end. In written Dutch you can spot separable verbs because they're listed as 'op|staan' or 'op staan' in dictionaries.
phrases.pronunciation: BEL-len: ik BEL op, aan-ZET-ten: ik ZET aan, UIT-leg-gen: ik LEG owt, MEE-nemen: ik NAYM may

phrases.hindi: omdat ik moe ben. omdat hij niet werkt. omdat wij Nederlands leren.
phrases.english: because I am tired. because he doesn't work. because we are learning Dutch.
phrases.context: CRITICAL: in subordinate clauses (after 'omdat', 'dat', 'als', 'want', etc.), the verb goes to the END. 'Ik ben moe' → 'omdat ik moe BEN'. The subject stays after the conjunction, verb goes last.
phrases.pronunciation: OM-dat ik MOO BEN. OM-dat hay NEET WERKT. OM-dat way NAY-der-lants LAY-ren.

phrases.hindi: Ik denk dat hij morgen komt. Ik weet dat zij Nederlands spreekt.
phrases.english: I think that he comes tomorrow. I know that she speaks Dutch.
phrases.context: Full sentences with subordinate clauses. 'Dat' triggers verb-final: 'hij morgen KOMT' (he tomorrow comes). Notice: in the main clause ('ik denk'), normal V2. In the 'dat' clause, verb goes last.
phrases.pronunciation: ik DENK dat hay MOR-gen KOMT. ik WAYT dat zay NAY-der-lants SPRAAKT.

phrases.hindi: Spreekt u Nederlands? Heb jij een pen? Gaat hij mee?
phrases.english: Do you speak Dutch? Do you have a pen? Is he coming along?
phrases.context: Yes/no questions: just invert subject and verb. No 'do' construction needed. 'Jij spreekt' → 'Spreekt jij?' Remember: 'jij/je' after verb drops the -t: 'Spreek JIJ?' not 'Spreekt jij?'
phrases.pronunciation: SPRAAKT u NAY-der-lants? hep YAY uhn PEN? CHAAT hay MAY?

grammar_notes: V2 rule: Dutch verb is ALWAYS in second position in main clauses. Count positions from the left — the verb must be #2, no matter what's in position #1
Separable verbs in subordinate clauses: the prefix rejoins the verb at the end: 'omdat ik opsta' (because I get up) — 'opsta' written together
Verb cluster in subordinate clauses with modals: 'omdat hij kan werken' (because he can work) — the modal goes before the infinitive, both at the end
With perfect tense in subordinate clauses: 'omdat ik heb gewerkt' — auxiliary (heb) before past participle (gewerkt), both at end
culture_notes: The V2 rule makes Dutch news headlines confusing at first — they often start with a topic. Practice by noticing the verb in slot 2 whenever you read Dutch

'Want' vs 'omdat': both mean 'because', but 'want' uses normal word order (no verb-final), 'omdat' requires verb-final. 'Ik ben moe, want ik heb hard gewerkt' vs 'Ik ben moe omdat ik hard heb gewerkt'
Separable verbs are everywhere in Dutch — look for them in your dictionary and note the prefix

skill_breakdown.explanation: Position 1 = anything (subject, time, place, adverb). Position 2 = ALWAYS the conjugated verb. Position 3+ = subject (if not in pos.1), objects, infinitives, etc. This is the backbone of Dutch sentence structure.

more_examples.english: In Amsterdam live many people. (location=1, wonen=2)
That I don't know. (dat=1, weet=2, ik=3)

skill_breakdown.explanation: Identify a separable verb: it has a prefix (op, aan, uit, mee, terug, neer, in, etc.) that can split off. In main clauses the prefix goes to the very end. In subordinate clauses and infinitive forms, they rejoin.

more_examples.english: to arrive: the train arrives (aan goes to end)
to figure out: I figure it out
to help along: can you help? (in infinitive, stays together)
to come back: when are you coming back?

practice_prompt: You are a Dutch tutor drilling word order — the V2 rule, the rest-of-verb-at-end pattern, separable verbs, and subordinate-clause verb-final structure. Address the student with 'u'. Open with: 'Goedendag! Laten we de woordvolgorde oefenen. Eerste opdracht: bouw een zin met deze woorden: vandaag / ik / werken / thuis.' Give ONE task at a time and wait for the answer. Rotate four task types: (1) build a V2 sentence from given elements, (2) fix a wrongly-ordered sentence, (3) transform a main clause into a subordinate clause with 'omdat' or 'dat' (verb to the end), (4) split a separable verb correctly (e.g. 'opbellen' → 'Ik bel je morgen op'). Watch for: missing V2 inversion after a fronted time/place ('Vandaag ik werk' → wrong), separable prefix not moving to the end, subordinate clauses keeping V2, infinitive not at the end in modal sentences. Flag every mistake with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]. Mix all four patterns across 6-8 items. End with: 'Heel goed! De woordvolgorde wordt automatisch. Tot ziens.' Don't switch to English unless asked.

theory.intro: Dutch sentence structure looks like English at first glance but follows very different rules. The conjugated verb MUST be second (V2 rule), the rest of the verb phrase exits to the END of the clause, and subordinate clauses send the verb all the way to the end. This chapter walks through V2 inversion, separable verbs (where prefixes fly to the end of the sentence), and the subordinate-clause flip — the three patterns that define Dutch syntax.

sections.heading: V2 — the conjugated verb is always second
sections.body:
The most important rule in Dutch syntax is the V2 rule: in a main clause, the conjugated verb must sit in second position. Always. No matter what.

The trick is that "second position" means the second ELEMENT, not the second word. Anything can fill the first slot — the subject, a time expression, a place, even an object — but the moment that first element ends, the verb has to come right after. If the subject got bumped out of first place, it slides to position three. English doesn't do this — we say "Tomorrow I am going to Amsterdam" with the subject still in front of the verb. Dutch demands the verb come second.

examples.breakdown: Subject (Ik) = pos. 1, verb (ga) = pos. 2 — standard order
Time (Morgen) = pos. 1, verb (ga) = pos. 2, subject (ik) bumped to pos. 3
Place (Naar Amsterdam) = pos. 1, verb (ga) = pos. 2, subject (ik) to pos. 3

callout.body: Forgetting V2 inversion is THE giveaway of a foreign speaker. `Vandaag ik ga naar werk` sounds immediately wrong to a Dutch ear — it has to be `Vandaag ga ik naar werk`. The moment something else fills slot 1, the subject and verb flip.

sections.cutting_intro: Welcome! Let's start with the most important rule in Dutch — the verb always sits in second position. Always. Watch what happens when we move things around.

quick_check.question: In Dutch, what does "second position" mean in the V2 rule?
options: The second word of the sentence
The second element of the sentence
quick_check.explanation: Yes — V2 means the second ELEMENT, not the second word. `Naar Amsterdam` is one element (three words) and still counts as position 1, so the verb comes right after it.

sections.heading: Questions and inversion
sections.body:
V2 has a clean side effect: yes/no questions are just statements with the verb shifted to position 1. The subject then slides to position 2. No `do` helper needed — Dutch doesn't have one.

For wh-questions, the question word (waar, wanneer, waarom, hoe, wie) takes position 1, the verb stays in position 2, and the subject follows. Same V2 rule, just with the question word filling the first slot.

table.caption: Statement vs yes/no question vs wh-question

callout.body: Question words like `waar`, `wanneer`, `waarom`, `hoe`, and `wie` always go in position 1. The verb stays at position 2 right after. Once you internalize this, every Dutch question follows the same template.

sections.cutting_intro: Questions in Dutch are easy once you know V2 — just shift the verb to the front, or let a question word lead the way.

quick_check.question: How do you form a yes/no question in Dutch?
options: Add `do` before the subject like English
Put the verb in position 1 and the subject in position 2
quick_check.explanation: Right — Dutch has no `do` helper. You just flip the verb to position 1, push the subject to position 2, and you've got a question.

sections.heading: The "rest of the verb" goes to the end
sections.body:
Here's where Dutch starts to feel really different. When a verb phrase has multiple parts — a modal + infinitive, a perfect tense (helper + past participle), a future with `zullen` — Dutch splits them.

The conjugated part (the one that changes with the subject) stays in V2 position, second-in-line as always. But the rest of the verb — the infinitive, the past participle, the future-tense partner — gets sent to the very END of the clause. Everything else (objects, time, place, adverbs) sits in between.

examples.hindi: Ik kan dit boek niet lezen.
examples.breakdown: `kan` (modal) stays at pos. 2, infinitive `lezen` flies to the end
`heb` (auxiliary) at pos. 2, past participle `gelezen` at the end
`zal` (future aux) at pos. 2, infinitive `doen` at the end

examples.english: We want to eat pizza tonight.
examples.breakdown: `willen` at pos. 2, infinitive `eten` at the end, everything else in between

callout.body: When planning a Dutch sentence, identify the FINITE verb first — the one that conjugates with the subject — and put EVERYTHING ELSE at the end of the clause. Modals, infinitives, past participles, separable prefixes: they all get exiled to the back.

sections.cutting_intro: Dutch splits verb phrases in two. The conjugated bit stays second; the rest flies to the end. This pattern shows up everywhere.

quick_check.question: In `Ik heb dit boek gelezen`, where does the past participle `gelezen` sit?
options: At the end of the clause
Wherever you want — Dutch is flexible
quick_check.explanation: Yes! The conjugated `heb` stays at position 2, and `gelezen` (the past participle) gets pushed to the end. Same rule for modals + infinitive, future + infinitive, anything with two verb parts.

sections.heading: Separable verbs — the prefix flies to the end
sections.body:
Dutch has a huge family of verbs that look like one word in the dictionary but separate when used in the present or simple past. `Opbellen` (to call up) is really `op` + `bellen`. In a real sentence, the verb part conjugates and stays in V2 position; the prefix `op` shoots all the way to the end of the clause.

This is the same "rest of the verb" rule from the previous section — the prefix is just another piece of the verb that doesn't belong in slot 2. Common separable verbs: `aankomen` (arrive), `meegaan` (come along), `opstaan` (get up), `uitleggen` (explain), `terugkomen` (come back), `aanzetten` (turn on).

table.caption: Common separable verbs split in a real sentence
rows: De trein komt om 8 uur aan.
Ik sta elke dag vroeg op.

callout.body: The prefix only detaches when the verb is the finite/conjugated verb in a main clause. In modal or perfect constructions, it stays attached: `Ik wil opbellen` (I want to call up — `opbellen` stays whole as the infinitive), `Ik heb opgebeld` (I've called — past participle, also whole). Detachment is a present/simple-past thing only.

sections.cutting_intro: Some Dutch verbs are sneaky — they look like one word in the dictionary, but split apart when you use them. The prefix flies to the end of the sentence.

quick_check.question: In `Ik bel jou morgen op`, what's happening with the prefix `op`?
options: It's a typo — should be one word `opbel`
It's a separable prefix that flew to the end of the clause
quick_check.explanation: Correct! `Opbellen` is a separable verb. The finite `bel` stays in V2 position, and the prefix `op` detaches and goes to the end. Standard pattern for the whole separable-verb family.

sections.heading: Subordinate clauses — the verb goes ALL the way to the end
sections.body:
The third big pattern: subordinating conjunctions trigger a completely different word order in their clause. When you start a clause with `omdat` (because), `als` (if), `dat` (that), `wanneer` (when), `terwijl` (while), `hoewel` (although), the verb gets exiled to the very end of THAT clause.

The main clause still obeys V2. But the subordinate clause is verb-final: subject, objects, adverbs first, then the verb (and any verb partners) at the very end. If two clauses share a sentence, the main one follows V2 and the subordinate one follows verb-final. They each follow their own rule.

table.caption: Common subordinating conjunctions + verb-final examples
rows: Ik ga naar huis omdat ik moe ben.
Hij zei dat hij morgen komt.
Als het regent, blijf ik thuis.
Ik weet niet wanneer hij komt.
Ik werk hoewel ik ziek ben.

callout.body: When you see `omdat`, `als`, `dat`, or `wanneer`, mentally route every verb in that clause to the end. It's an automatic transform. The main clause keeps V2; the subordinate clause goes verb-final. Two clauses, two different rules, in the same sentence.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's the third big pattern. Certain conjunctions — `omdat`, `als`, `dat`, `wanneer` — send the verb all the way to the end of their clause. Watch.

quick_check.question: In `Ik ga naar huis omdat ik moe ben.`, why is `ben` at the very end?
options: Because Dutch always ends sentences with `ben`
Because `omdat` triggers verb-final word order in its clause
Because `moe` has to come before the verb
quick_check.explanation: Right — `omdat` is a subordinating conjunction, so the whole clause becomes verb-final. The verb `ben` gets exiled to the end. Same with `dat`, `als`, `wanneer`, `terwijl`, `hoewel`.

theory.wrap_up: Word order is what makes Dutch feel structurally different from English. Master V2 first — verb-second, always, no exceptions in main clauses. Then internalize the "rest-of-verb at end" rule: modals, perfect tense, separable prefixes all exile their partner verb-parts to the back of the clause. Finally, learn the subordinate-clause flip: `omdat`, `als`, `dat`, `wanneer` all send the verb to the very end. Once these three patterns click, you can build any Dutch sentence. The phrases below mix all three to drill the transformations until they feel automatic.
